summary: Sentry's issue detail screen provides a dense but well-organized layout for debugging. Key concerns include information overload without progressive disclosure, small contrast issues on secondary text, and a promotional banner that competes with critical debugging context. The activity feed and right sidebar could benefit from clearer visual hierarchy.

issues.title: Low contrast on secondary/metadata text
issues.location: Metadata labels like 'handled', 'level', 'url', 'Trace: Trace ID' in the Highlights section and timestamps in Activity
issues.problem: Multiple secondary text elements (e.g., 'handled', 'level', 'url', timestamp labels) appear in a muted gray that may not meet the 4.5:1 minimum contrast ratio against the white background. Users with low vision will struggle to read these critical debugging details.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 1.4.3 requires normal-sized text to have at least a 4.5:1 contrast ratio. The muted gray labels appear to fall below this threshold, making essential metadata inaccessible.
issues.recommendation: Increase the color of all secondary text labels to at least #595959 on white backgrounds to ensure a 4.5:1 ratio. Use font weight or size differentiation rather than reduced contrast alone to denote secondary hierarchy.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.4.3 Contrast Minimum (AA): normal text requires 4.5:1 contrast ratio

issues.title: Color-only status indicators for 'Unhandled' and 'New' badges
issues.location: Status badges ('Unhandled' in red, 'New' in gray) below the issue title
issues.problem: The 'Unhandled' badge relies heavily on its red color to communicate urgency, while 'New' uses a neutral gray. Users with color vision deficiencies (deuteranopia, protanopia) may not distinguish the severity difference between these badges. No icon or pattern supplements the color coding.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 1.4.1 prohibits using color as the sole means of conveying information. Color-blind users cannot differentiate urgency levels when the only distinguishing factor is hue.
issues.recommendation: Add a warning icon (e.g., ⚠) to the 'Unhandled' badge or use a distinct shape/border style. Ensure each status has a non-color differentiator such as an icon prefix or distinct border pattern.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.4.1 Use of Color (A): information must not be conveyed by color alone

issues.title: Icon-only buttons lack accessible labels
issues.location: Action bar icons — bell/notification, share/export, and '...' overflow menu next to Resolve/Archive
issues.problem: The bell icon, export/share icon, and ellipsis overflow button in the action bar appear to be icon-only with no visible text labels. Without aria-labels or tooltips, screen reader users cannot determine their purpose, and sighted users must guess their function.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 1.1.1 requires all non-text content to have a text alternative. Icon-only controls without accessible names create barriers for assistive technology users and violate the principle of clear affordance.
issues.recommendation: Add aria-label attributes to each icon button (e.g., 'Notifications', 'Share issue', 'More actions'). Also add visible tooltips on hover/focus to aid all users.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.1.1 Non-text Content (A): all non-text content must have a text alternative

issues.title: Excessive information density creates cognitive overload
issues.location: Main content area — Highlights, Stack Trace, event chart, tags, and Git provider banner all visible simultaneously
issues.problem: The issue detail screen presents event metadata, a chart, highlights, stack trace, and a promotional banner all in a single scrollable view. This creates a very high cognitive load, making it difficult for users to focus on the most relevant debugging information. The promotional 'Connect with Git Providers' banner further competes for attention.
issues.critical_reason: Miller's Law suggests working memory can hold roughly 7±2 items. The simultaneous presentation of 8+ distinct information blocks exceeds typical cognitive capacity, slowing task completion and increasing errors.
issues.recommendation: Use progressive disclosure — collapse the Highlights and event chart by default and let users expand them. Move the Git provider promotion to a less prominent position (e.g., settings page or a subtle inline hint) rather than a large card within the debugging flow.
issues.reference: Universal Principles of Design — Progressive Disclosure: show only what is needed at each step

issues.title: Promotional banner disrupts task flow
issues.location: 'Connect with Git Providers' card with illustration near bottom of main content
issues.problem: A large promotional card for Git provider integration interrupts the stack trace debugging workflow. It includes a decorative illustration that takes significant vertical space, pushing critical stack trace frames below the fold. This is a non-essential CTA placed within a high-focus debugging context.
issues.critical_reason: Signal-to-noise ratio is degraded when promotional content is interspersed with primary task content. Users engaged in debugging have high task focus, and interruptions reduce efficiency and cause frustration.
issues.recommendation: Move this promotion to a dismissible toast, an onboarding checklist, or the project settings page. If kept inline, reduce its size significantly and allow permanent dismissal. The decorative illustration should be removed or minimized.
issues.reference: Universal Principles of Design — Signal-to-Noise Ratio: maximise relevant information, minimise clutter

issues.title: Small touch/click targets on navigation tabs
issues.location: Event navigation tabs: 'Highlights', 'Stack Trace', 'Replay', 'Breadcrumbs', 'Logs', 'Trace…' horizontal tab bar
issues.problem: The horizontal tab labels for navigating within the event (Highlights, Stack Trace, Replay, etc.) appear to have small clickable areas with tight spacing. On touch devices or for users with motor impairments, these targets may be difficult to activate accurately.
issues.critical_reason: WCAG 2.5.8 (Target Size Minimum) requires interactive targets to be at least 24×24 CSS pixels. Densely packed text links without adequate padding fall below this threshold and increase misclick rates.
issues.recommendation: Increase the padding around each tab to ensure at least 24×24px target size, ideally 44×44px for touch. Add clear visual separators or spacing between tabs to reduce accidental activation of adjacent items.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 2.5.8 Target Size Minimum (AA): interactive targets must be at least 24x24 CSS pixels

issues.title: Ambiguous 'Seer' AI feature labeling
issues.location: Right sidebar — 'Seer' section with warning icon and 'Initial Guess' label
issues.problem: The 'Seer' feature uses a brand name that doesn't clearly communicate its purpose (AI-assisted root cause analysis). The term 'Initial Guess' further undermines user confidence in the suggestion. New users cannot quickly understand what this section does or how reliable it is.
issues.critical_reason: Nielsen's Heuristic #2 (Match Between System and Real World) requires using language familiar to users. Opaque brand names and tentative labels like 'Initial Guess' create a gulf of evaluation — users cannot assess the value of the information.
issues.recommendation: Rename 'Seer' to something descriptive like 'AI Root Cause Analysis' or add a subtitle. Replace 'Initial Guess' with 'AI Analysis' or 'Suggested Root Cause' with a confidence indicator to set proper expectations.
issues.reference: The Design of Everyday Things (Norman) — Gulf of Evaluation: user cannot tell if their action worked

issues.title: Activity feed lacks clear visual grouping
issues.location: Right sidebar — Activity section showing Unresolved/Archived status changes
issues.problem: The activity feed lists status changes (Unresolved, Archived, Unresolved) with identical visual treatment and minimal spacing. The repeated 'Unresolved' entries are visually indistinguishable at a glance, and the crossed-out icon for status changes is subtle. Users must read each entry carefully to understand the issue's lifecycle.
issues.critical_reason: Gestalt's Law of Similarity suggests that items with identical visual treatment are perceived as equivalent. Without distinct visual cues for different action types, users cannot quickly scan and differentiate status changes.
issues.recommendation: Use distinct background colors or left-border colors for different activity types (e.g., green for resolved, orange for archived, red for unresolved). Group related status changes and add clearer iconography to differentiate action types at a glance.

issues.title: Dropdown filter labels lack visible selected state clarity
issues.location: Filter bar — 'All Envs' and 'Since First Seen' dropdown buttons
issues.problem: The filter dropdowns 'All Envs' and 'Since First Seen' use plain text with small chevrons. When default values are selected, there is no visual indication that these are active filters versus labels. Users may not realize they can refine the view or that the current view is already filtered.
issues.critical_reason: Norman's Signifier principle states that visible cues must clearly indicate where users can act and what state the interface is in. Ambiguous filter controls reduce discoverability and awareness of current context.
issues.recommendation: Add a subtle background fill or border to indicate these are interactive dropdowns. When a non-default filter is active, use a badge count or distinct highlight color to signal the active filter state.
issues.reference: The Design of Everyday Things (Norman) — Signifier: visible cues must indicate where to act

issues.title: Truncated tag values reduce scannability
issues.location: Right side of event chart — tag breakdown showing '…ntry-example-page' for url
issues.problem: The url tag value is truncated to '…ntry-example-page', hiding the meaningful prefix of the URL. Users debugging an issue need to see the full URL at a glance to quickly identify the affected page without additional interaction.
issues.critical_reason: Don't Make Me Think (Krug) emphasizes that users scan rather than read. Truncated values that remove the most distinctive leading portion of a string force users to hover or click for essential information, adding unnecessary interaction cost.
issues.recommendation: Use middle-truncation (e.g., 'http://…/sentry-example-page') or expand the tag area width. Alternatively, show the path portion only since localhost:3000 is less distinctive than the page path.
issues.reference: Don't Make Me Think (Krug) — Billboard Design 101: users scan, not read
